summary: Cantrill celebrates DTrace's tenth anniversary by cataloging the significant developments in the DTrace ecosystem over the past five years, including ports to Linux, new language bindings, Brendan Gregg's authoritative book, and community-driven improvements. He reflects on how DTrace and illumos survived Sun's collapse and Oracle's re-closing of Solaris, crediting the resilience of open source communities. The post serves as both a retrospective on technical achievements and an acknowledgment of the many contributors who expanded DTrace's reach and capabilities.
key_points: DTrace was ported to Linux twice and extended to support dynamic languages including node.js, Lua, Perl, and Ruby
Brendan Gregg's DTrace book became the canonical practitioner's guide
The DTrace community survived Sun's collapse and Oracle's re-closing of Solaris through open source principles
DTrace unconferences (dtrace.conf) established a regular community gathering on an 'Olympiad cadence'
User-land types in DTrace represents the next major frontier for development
money_quotes: If you don't know by now, this is often correctly translated as 'I really, really don't feel like it.'
It's a testament to the power of open source and open communities that DTrace has – along with its operating system of record, illumos – emerged from these events empowered and invigorated.
The most surprising development to my past-self may be how smoothly DTrace and its community survived the collapse of Sun and the craven re-closing of Solaris.
The International DTrace Committee will soon be selecting the host city for dtrace.conf(16), so get those bribes ready!
key_insight: Open source communities can not only survive but thrive after corporate upheaval, emerging stronger when built on genuine technical collaboration and shared purpose.
